other_observers: Brenda went back to get her camera and therefore failed to see the bird. After she returned we looked for the warbler for about an hour but could not find it.; Yes
specific_location: About three miles south of Glade Lake on Forest Road 504
description: Warbler was with other warbler species and Chipping Sparrows. The warbler was about the same size as the sparrows and two Nashville Warblers bathing in the same waterhole. The warbler was somewhat smaller then the Yellow-rumps. What first caught my eye was the yellow-orange color of the head and throat. The warbler had a very pronounced yellow-orange eyebrow which extended from the bill to the neck. Infact the hole face was yellow-orange except the light brown area of the auriculars and lores. The bird had two very bold, white wing bars and faint streaks of dark gray on the flanks. The belly and undertail coverts were off white. I was above the bird part of the time and could see the head, back and upper tail. The bird did have pale streaking on the back. The head was uniform gray as was the tail.Behaviors: The warbler was bathing with several other small birds. They would fly up into a small aspen then back to the water for another quick splash about before again preening and drying off in the aspen. A culvert under the road had created the water hole the birds were using to bath. The water was colored red from the clay but did not affect the plumage color of the birds. The two Nashville Warblers yellow colors were still yellow.Call: None heardPlumage: Could have been a HY male?
